mnemonics.title: Remember Memory Segments
mnemonics.content: THDBS (Think Hard, Drink Beer, Study) - Text, Heap, Data, BSS, Stack - The 5 segments of C program memory layout in order from code to dynamic storage.

mnemonics.title: Stack Direction
mnemonics.content: SAD - Stack Adds Down. Remember: Stack grows downward (to lower addresses) like a stack of plates being added at the bottom.

mnemonics.title: Heap Direction
mnemonics.content: HAU - Heap Adds Up. Remember: Heap grows upward (to higher addresses) like filling a bucket from the bottom up.

mnemonics.title: Variable Storage Locations
mnemonics.content: GLoSS - Global in Data (or BSS), Local in Stack. Static goes to Data/BSS, Automatic goes to Stack.

acronyms.usage: Uninitialized global and static variables are stored in BSS segment, which is zero-initialized by the OS.
VLAs are allocated on the Stack at runtime, unlike fixed-size arrays which are also on Stack.
OS structure containing process information including memory layout details.
Security attack exploiting return addresses stored in Stack.
Security technique that randomizes memory segment addresses to prevent exploits.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a vertical column: Text at bottom (lowest addresses), then Data, then BSS. Above those is Heap growing upward. At the top (highest addresses) is Stack growing downward. They meet in the middle like two climbers approaching each other.

Think of a stack frame as a 'function envelope' - it contains: return address on top (like destination address on an envelope), then parameters, then space for return value, then saved registers, then local variables at the bottom.
Imagine Heap as a building under construction going up floor by floor, while Stack is like a crane lowering sections from above. They work toward each other.
Pointers themselves are variables, so they follow the same rules as other variables: global pointers in Data/BSS, local pointers in Stack, dynamically allocated pointer targets in Heap.

analogies.analogy: The Heap is like a parking lot with no assigned spaces. You request a specific number of spots (malloc), get a pointer to your spot, and you must remember to release it (free) or it's wasted forever.
The Stack is like a stack of trays in a cafeteria - you add (push) and remove (pop) only from the top. Each function call adds a new tray, and returning removes it.

Think of a program like a building: Text is the architectural blueprints (immutable), Data and BSS are the permanent fixtures (furniture), Heap is the storage unit you rent (temporary), Stack is the temporary workspace (active during operations).
